name: Algebra & Functions
description: Surds, indices, quadratics, inequalities, simultaneous equations, proof by deduction and contradiction
commonMisconceptions: Squaring both sides of an inequality always preserves the direction
The discriminant b^2 - 4ac > 0 means exactly two roots (ignoring repeated roots when = 0)
sqrt(a + b) = sqrt(a) + sqrt(b)
x^0 = 0 instead of x^0 = 1
Proof by example is a valid form of mathematical proof

name: Polynomials
description: Factor theorem, algebraic division, partial fractions, binomial expansion
commonMisconceptions: The factor theorem says f(a) = 0 means (x + a) is a factor (should be (x - a))
Forgetting to check for improper fractions before decomposing into partial fractions
The binomial expansion of (1 + x)^n is valid for all x when n is not a positive integer
Ignoring the remainder when dividing polynomials

name: Coordinate Geometry
description: Equations of lines and circles, parametric equations, converting between Cartesian and parametric forms
commonMisconceptions: The gradient of a perpendicular line is just the negative, not the negative reciprocal
Forgetting to halve the coefficients when completing the square to find the centre of a circle
Assuming a tangent to a circle passes through the centre
Confusing dy/dx with dy/dt when differentiating parametric equations (must use chain rule: dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt))
Forgetting that parametric equations can trace only part of a Cartesian curve depending on the domain of the parameter

name: Sequences & Series
description: Arithmetic and geometric sequences and series, sigma notation, convergence, and the binomial expansion for rational n
commonMisconceptions: Using r instead of d in arithmetic series formulae (or vice versa)
A geometric series converges for |r| <= 1, forgetting that r = 1 causes divergence
Confusing the nth term formula with the sum formula
Assuming all geometric series have a sum to infinity
Off-by-one errors when counting the number of terms between two values

name: Trigonometry
description: Trig ratios, identities, equations, graphs, radians, arc length and sector area
commonMisconceptions: sin(A + B) = sin(A) + sin(B)
The inverse of sin is 1/sin rather than arcsin
Forgetting to find all solutions in the given range (missing quadrants)
Using degrees in formulas that require radians (arc length, sector area)
tan(90) = 0 instead of undefined

name: Exponentials & Logarithms
description: Laws of logarithms, exponential functions, natural logarithm, solving exponential equations
commonMisconceptions: log(a + b) = log(a) + log(b) — this is wrong; only log(ab) = log(a) + log(b)
ln(0) = 0 instead of being undefined
e is exactly 2.7 rather than an irrational constant approximately 2.71828
log_a(0) = 0 instead of being undefined
Confusing ln(x^2) with (ln x)^2

name: Differentiation
description: First principles, power rule, chain/product/quotient rules, tangents, normals, stationary points
commonMisconceptions: d/dx(x^n) = x^(n-1) — forgetting the coefficient n
The product rule is d/dx(uv) = (du/dx)(dv/dx) instead of u(dv/dx) + v(du/dx)
A stationary point where f''(x) = 0 must be a point of inflection
The normal has the same gradient as the tangent
Forgetting to use the chain rule for composite functions like sin(3x)

name: Integration
description: Reverse of differentiation, definite integrals, area under a curve, integration by substitution and by parts
commonMisconceptions: Forgetting the + c constant in indefinite integration
Integrating x^n gives x^(n+1) without dividing by (n+1)
Area below the x-axis is automatically positive when calculated by integration
The trapezium rule gives an exact answer rather than an approximation
Integrating 1/x gives ln|x| but forgetting the absolute value

name: Numerical Methods
description: Location of roots, iterative methods, Newton-Raphson, numerical integration using the trapezium rule

subskills.name: Location of Roots (Change of Sign)
keywords: f(a) and f(b) differ in sign

commonMisconceptions: A change of sign always means there is exactly one root in the interval (there could be multiple or a discontinuity)
An iterative formula will always converge to a root regardless of the starting value
Newton-Raphson cannot fail (it can diverge or oscillate if the starting point is poor or f'(x) is near zero)
More trapezium strips always give an overestimate (depends on concavity of the curve)
Forgetting to state the condition f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs AND f is continuous on the interval

name: Vectors
description: 2D and 3D vectors, position vectors, magnitude and direction, geometric proofs using vectors
commonMisconceptions: Confusing AB with BA (the direction matters; AB = b - a, not a - b)
Assuming two vectors are parallel because they have the same magnitude
Forgetting that collinear points require the vectors to be parallel AND share a common point
Calculating the magnitude of a 3D vector as sqrt(x^2 + y^2) instead of sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)

name: Statistical Sampling
description: Sampling methods including random, systematic, stratified, quota and opportunity; understanding bias and generalisability
commonMisconceptions: A larger sample always eliminates bias (sample size helps precision, not bias)
Systematic sampling is the same as stratified sampling
A census is always more accurate than a sample (it can still contain measurement errors)
Random sampling means haphazard or unplanned selection

name: Data Presentation & Interpretation
description: Histograms, box plots, cumulative frequency, scatter diagrams, measures of central tendency and spread, correlation and regression
commonMisconceptions: The y-axis on a histogram shows frequency rather than frequency density
Standard deviation can be negative
Correlation implies causation
Extrapolation from a regression line is as reliable as interpolation
The mean is always the best measure of central tendency (not for skewed data)

name: Probability
description: Conditional probability, tree diagrams, Venn diagrams, mutually exclusive and independent events, set notation

subskills.name: Independence & Mutual Exclusivity
keywords: P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B)

commonMisconceptions: P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B) is always true (only valid for independent events)
Events can be both mutually exclusive and independent (only possible if P(A) or P(B) is 0)
Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A)
Assuming 'without replacement' does not affect probabilities
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) without subtracting P(A and B)

name: Statistical Distributions
description: Binomial distribution, normal distribution, using distribution tables and calculators, approximations
commonMisconceptions: The normal distribution can take negative values, so X ~ N(5, 4) can give values less than 0 (students assume it cannot)
The variance is sigma, not sigma^2 (confusing standard deviation and variance in N(mu, sigma^2))
Binomial distribution can be used when probability changes between trials
P(X = a) for a continuous distribution is some positive value (it is always 0)
Forgetting to use the continuity correction when approximating binomial with normal (when required)

name: Statistical Hypothesis Testing
description: Hypothesis tests for binomial and normal distributions, critical values, critical regions, p-values, significance levels
commonMisconceptions: Rejecting H0 proves the alternative hypothesis is true (it only provides evidence)
The significance level is the probability that H0 is true
A result that is not significant means H0 is definitely true
Confusing one-tailed and two-tailed critical regions (halving the significance level incorrectly)
Writing 'accept H0' instead of 'do not reject H0' or 'insufficient evidence to reject H0'

name: Kinematics
description: Constant acceleration (suvat), velocity-time and displacement-time graphs, variable acceleration using calculus, projectiles
commonMisconceptions: Using suvat equations when acceleration is not constant
Forgetting that acceleration due to gravity acts downwards (sign errors with g = 9.8)
Assuming horizontal and vertical components of a projectile affect each other
The area under a displacement-time graph gives velocity (it does not; the gradient does)
Confusing velocity with speed (velocity has direction)

name: Forces & Newton's Laws
description: Force diagrams, equilibrium, Newton's three laws, F = ma, connected particles, friction, inclined planes
commonMisconceptions: Confusing mass and weight (weight = mg, not just m)
Assuming friction always equals mu R (it equals mu R only at the point of sliding; otherwise F <= mu R)
Forgetting to resolve forces parallel and perpendicular to an inclined plane
Using the same value of a for both particles in a connected system without considering the string constraint
Newton's third law pairs act on the same body (they act on different bodies)

name: Moments
description: Turning effects, principle of moments, equilibrium of rigid bodies, centre of mass, tilting and toppling
commonMisconceptions: Moment = force x distance (forgetting it must be the perpendicular distance from the line of action to the pivot)
The weight of a uniform beam acts at the support point rather than at its centre
Forgetting to include all forces when taking moments (especially the weight of the beam itself)
Confusing tilting (about a point) with toppling (centre of mass beyond the base)
